title: Fern Walk
desc: The marsh path disappears under new growth unless someone cuts it back every day.

steps.text:
The fern walk across Everrain's marshy quarter is less a bridge than a daily agreement. Reeds are laid over narrow supports, side cords are tied from peg to peg, and every morning the dripping green around it tries to take the whole structure back.

When you arrive, the path is still there, but only technically. Broad wet fronds lean over the rail lines. Moss has swallowed the first few planks. A worker with a hooked knife and a bundle of fresh reed matting jerks a thumb at the mess. "If we leave it like this," she says, "someone misses the next turn, steps off thinking it is still path, and spends the afternoon wetter than planned."

You can clear the overgrowth from the sides, or you can work the middle and retie the reed mat where last night's rain loosened it.

options.label: Retie the reed mat and side cords

steps.text:
The hooked knife slips under the biggest fronds with a wet hiss. You cut only what has crossed the path itself, leaving the rest to drip and shimmer in place. The air fills with a sharp green scent every time a stem comes free, and cold drops slide down your sleeves whenever you reach too far into the canopy.

As the sides clear, the walk changes character. What had felt like a tunnel of blind damp becomes a route again, with sightlines to the next platform and the next hanging lamp. A line of tiny pale lizards that had been basking on the hand cord blinks at your interruption and relocates with obvious offense.

The worker nods along the fresh opening. "Better. Now people can admire the marsh without disappearing into it." In Everrain, that is the usual definition of good maintenance.

options.label: Stack the cut fronds and clear the way

steps.text:
The reed mat has drifted sideways in three places and bowed at the center where the supports softened overnight. You kneel along the walk, drawing fresh tie cords through the reed weave and around the side pegs while the worker braces the far end with one boot.

It is damp, repetitive labor, but satisfying in the way all necessary things are. Pull, knot, test. Pull, knot, test. By the time you reach the far platform, the path no longer slouches. It lies flat and springy over the marsh, ready for boots instead of accidents.

The worker gives the central span a solid stomp, listens to the supports answer, and grins. "Good. If anyone falls in now, at least it will be because they did something interesting." She says this with the calm confidence of someone who has seen every ordinary mistake the marsh can offer.

options.label: Leave the walk fit for traffic
